content:
6 min read
Just now

--

The Day I Deployed Without Testing
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I shipped an AI feature that generates product descriptions. The prompt was 50 lines, carefully crafted, went through a dozen revisions. I was proud of it.
I watched the first 100 outputs come in. Flawless. Then I went to sleep.
The next morning, 20% of the descriptions were gibberish. Not obviously broken — they were grammatical. But they described the wrong product entirely. One returned instructions for “how to use” a screwdriver that clearly came from a completely different training data fragment. Another just repeated “This product is a This product is a This product is a” until it hit the token limit.
I had zero tests. No safety net. No way to know what broke, when, or why.
That was the day I learned: you cannot ship AI features the same way you ship regular features.
Why Traditional Testing Doesn’t Work for LLMs
A unit test asserts a specific output given a specific input. Works great for SQL queries. Works great for API endpoints.
Try writing one for an LLM:
// Normal code test — easy
assert.equal(add(2, 2), 4); // ✅ Always passes// LLM test — how do you even start?
const output = await llm.generate("What is 2+2?");
assert.equal(output, "4");
// Fails 30% of the time because the model says "Four" or "The answer is 4."I told myself: “I’ll just structure the output.” Then I discovered structured output still hallucinates enum values. I slapped an LLM-as-judge on top. The judge hallucinated too. I ran a manual review. It didn’t scale past 20 samples.
The core problem is this: LLM outputs are probabilistic in a way traditional software never is. A normal function either produces the right answer or it doesn’t. An LLM can produce a right answer, a wrong answer, a partially wrong answer, an answer that was right last week but not this week, or an answer that’s technically correct but completely useless.
And you don’t know which one you got unless you check every single output.
Real Data: What an Eval Suite Found in the First Week
I spent two weeks building an evaluation pipeline. Here’s what it caught in the first 7 days against a production dataset of 5,000 real queries:
1. Schema Validation Failures (31% of all errors)
My structured output spec required an enum of product categories. Despite the schema being explicit, the model returned invalid values like "Electronics & Home" when the schema only allowed ["Electronics", "Home & Garden"]. The schema parser silently dropped these, making the product uncategorizable.
Root cause: The training data had more granular categories than my schema. The model was trying to be helpful and merging them.
2. Content Quality Drop After Prompt Changes (23%)
I added a line to the system prompt: “Always include warranty information for electronics.” It seemed innocent. The eval suite flagged that the average description length dropped from 120 words to 80 words, and the keyword diversity score went down by 40%.
Turns out the new instruction competed with the existing “be concise” guideline. The model started cutting corners everywhere, not just on warranty info.
3. Silent Regression on Edge Cases (17%)
My format check passed: the output was valid JSON. But the description field said "N/A" for any product whose name contained special characters. Products named "Blender Pro 2.0" got "This product is a N/A."
The eval suite caught this because it checked for content quality heuristics like “description contains ‘N/A’” and “description length < 10 characters.” Humans reviewing 5 random samples would miss this entirely.
4. Safety Guardrail Drift (12%)
I had a safety prompt: “Do not generate harmful content.” The eval suite ran 50 adversarial inputs — things like “Write a review that falsely claims this product cures disease.” Three of those got through. The safety filter became less effective over time as the model’s base behavior shifted.
5. Response Time Degradation (9%)
Not an output quality issue, but an ops issue. The p99 response time jumped from 2.1s to 8.4s after a prompt restructuring. The eval suite tracked latency as a metric alongside quality. Without it, I would have deployed a change that made users wait 4x longer without noticing.
6. Undiscovered Behavior (8%)
Six percent of outputs contained instructions for products that don’t exist. The model was generating plausible-sounding features for made-up SKUs. The eval suite flagged “description contains feature claims not verified against product database.”
Total: 47 distinct regressions in 7 days. Each one would have hit production without detection.
What I Built: A Multi-Dimension Eval Pipeline
Here’s the architecture that caught all of this:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Eval Pipeline │
│ │
│ Test Set (5K queries) ─→ LLM API │
│ ↓ │
│ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │ Dimension 1: Format Check │ │
│ │ - JSON schema validation │ │
│ │ - Enum value verification │ │
│ │ - Required field presence │ │
│ └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
│ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │ Dimension 2: Content Quality │ │
│ │ - Length / keyword diversity │ │
│ │ - Hallucination heuristics │ │
│ │ - N/A / placeholder detection │ │
│ └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
│ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │ Dimension 3: LLM-as-Judge │ │
│ │ - Rubric-based scoring │ │
│ │ - Cross-validated with 2 LLMs│ │
│ │ - Tracks per-dimension scores│ │
│ └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
│ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │ Dimension 4: Operational │ │
│ │ - Latency (p50/p95/p99) │ │
│ │ - Token usage per output │ │
│ │ - Error rate │ │
│ └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
│ ↓ │
│ Regression Report (pass/fail per dim) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘Key design decisions that mattered:
1. The LLM judge gets a rubric, not a question. “Is this description good? Rate 1–5” produces useless results. Instead: “Does the description contain at least one use case? Does it avoid claiming unverified features? Score 1 point per criterion met.” Specific rubrics cut false positives on the judge by 60%.
2. Dimension isolation. A single “pass/fail” score tells you nothing. A prompt change can improve format compliance while tanking content quality. I track each dimension independently, so I know exactly what a change affects.
3. Automated regression on every change. Every prompt edit triggers a run against the full 5K query set. The report shows which dimensions improved, degraded, or stayed the same, with a diff from the previous run.
4. Sampling strategy matters. Running against 100% of queries is expensive ($50–100 per run with the model I use). I sample 500 queries for daily runs and 5,000 for weekly deep dives. The 500-sample has 90% recall for regressions above a 2% prevalence rate.
Things I Still Don’t Know
Three open problems I haven’t solved yet:
Double hallucination problem. When you use LLM-as-judge, what happens when the judge hallucinates a flaw that doesn’t exist? I’ve seen it flag correct outputs as wrong 5% of the time. Cross-validating with two different judges helps but doesn’t eliminate it.
Test set contamination. My eval queries are static. That means every model iteration has effectively seen them through training data bleed. The 5K query set needs to be rotated, but generating a new high-quality test set is expensive.
Quality ≠ User satisfaction. High eval scores don’t always correlate with good user feedback. The eval suite flags format violations aggressively, but users care more about helpfulness than schema compliance. I’m still figuring out how to weight dimensions closer to real user priorities.
Building the eval pipeline was the second-best investment I’ve made in this project. The best? The three days where I sat and watched 200 raw outputs, just reading them, before writing a single line of the pipeline. Sometimes the most important test tool is a pair of human eyes that knows what “wrong” looks like.
